nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, rice paddies, ditches, and the muddy margins of slow-moving streams and ponds across temperate and tropical Asia. Highly tolerant of seasonal water-level fluctuation, it grows in both emersed form — above the waterline, producing rounder marsh leaves — and submersed form, where the leaves become narrow and often take on a pink or red hue.

decorPreferences: open background or midground bush placement
bright lighting and iron dosing for pink-red coloration

summary: Rotala rotundifolia, commonly called Dwarf Rotala or Pink Rotala, is one of the most widely kept red-pink stem plants in the planted aquarium hobby. It grows quickly, tolerates a broad range of water conditions, and develops vivid pink to red coloration under bright light with iron dosing and low nitrate levels.
careNotes: Rotala rotundifolia is among the most widely kept stem plants in the hobby and is generally considered one of the easiest red-pink stems to grow. Despite the Latin species name (which refers to the rounded leaves of the marsh-grown emersed form), the submerged aquarium form has narrow, needle-like leaves that range from yellow-green under low light to vivid pink and red under high light. The famous deep pink-red colour is most reliably achieved by the 'nitrate limit' method: keep NO3 around 5-10 ppm while dosing iron and a full micronutrient mix; high-PAR lighting and CO2 amplify the effect. Many popular trade variants exist: 'Green' is the standard green-leaved form; 'Red' (also sold as 'Pink') is the most common pink-flushing form; 'Colorata' is a richly coloured cultivar; 'Blood Red' is a more demanding deep-red cultivar that wants high light and CO2; 'H'ra' has feathery pine-needle-like foliage with orange-pink tones; 'Orange Juice' is an orange-yellow morph. Important name confusion: 'Rotala indica' is sometimes used as a trade name for R. rotundifolia, but the true Rotala indica is a separate sister species. Similarly, 'Rotala Vietnam' and 'Rotala wallichii' are related but distinct species, not variants of R. rotundifolia. Placement is background or trimmed-bush midground; the plant produces side shoots willingly and becomes bushier with frequent trimming. Lower leaves shade out and lose colour without regular pruning. Avoid drastic parameter swings — like other delicate stem plants, R. rotundifolia can melt if moved between very different water chemistries.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings — cut any portion of the stem (head cuttings of 10 cm or more work best) and replant directly into the substrate, where roots and side shoots develop quickly. Pinching off the apical bud encourages the mother stem to throw multiple lateral shoots, which can be trimmed off and replanted for rapid mass propagation. Floating cuttings will also root in the water column before planting. The plant can also be grown emersed for visually different (rounder-leaved) growth and is occasionally propagated this way in nurseries.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Used for species identity, family/origin (South-East Asia), height (15-30+ cm), growth rate (medium), light demand (low, with note that good light produces red leaves), CO2 demand (low), care difficulty (easy), placement (stem - side shoots, prune for compact form), and trade-name overlap with 'Rotala indica'. The supplied search URL (tropica.com/en/search?q=Rotala+rotundifolia) returned no per-species detail; resolved via search to the canonical product detail page id 4447.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Confirms family Lythraceae, origin South-East Asia, height 4-8" (~10-20 cm), pH 6.5-7.5, fast growth, easy-to-moderate care, background placement, high light + CO2 (20-30 ppm) for best red colour, propagation by stem cuttings, sensitivity to parameter swings (melt risk).

additional.name: 2HR Aquarist
additional.notes: Confirms easy care, hardy across wide parameter range, fast growth, grows reasonably red without CO2 but CO2 drives branching and density. Documents the 'nitrate limit' technique for driving deep red coloration (low NO3 + iron + micronutrients). Page returned 403 on direct WebFetch but search-result excerpt captured key claims.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-source confirmation of temperature 20-28 °C, pH 6.0-8.0, gH 2-12, lighting 35-50 PAR (medium-high), CO2 optional, height 5-30+ cm, propagation by head cuttings (≥10 cm) and runners, native countries (Japan, China, Taiwan, Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, India). Also documents R. rotundifolia vs R. indica species confusion.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (R. rotundifolia 'H'ra' variant)
additional.notes: Documents the 'H'ra' cultivar: feathery pine-needle-like foliage, orange-pink coloration under high light + CO2, midground or background placement, creeping side-shoot growth. Used to enrich the trade-variant list in alsoKnownAs and careNotes.
